Paul begins Romans 13 with a stark declaration: government authority comes from God. Not just good government. Not just government we agree with. All authority that exists has been appointed by God, and to resist it is to resist what God has set in place. This hits hard because we live in an age of questioning authority, and rightfully so in many contexts. But Paul doesn't qualify his statement with exceptions. He calls Christians to submit—not blindly, but genuinely—recognizing that even the magistrates of pagan Rome, even Nero's regime, stood within God's sovereign arrangement of human affairs. Submission matters not merely to avoid punishment, though that is real, but as a matter of conscience. When you pay your taxes, when you render respect, when you obey laws that are just, you are acknowledging God's ordering of society. This is not peripheral Christianity. It is basic civic obedience rooted in your relationship with the God who holds all things together.

Yet Paul immediately pivots to something deeper than mere rule-following. After establishing submission to authority, he moves to the law of love—the commandment that sums everything up: love your neighbor as yourself. The shift is not accidental. A Christian's primary obligation is not to the state but to love, and love "does no wrong to its neighbor." Here is the tension the Christian must hold: you submit to government and pay taxes, but you also recognize that love is the fulfillment of all law. If a government commands what love forbids, or forbids what love commands, the conscience awakens to a higher loyalty. Paul doesn't spell this out here, but the principle is present. Your submission to earthly authority is always subordinate to your allegiance to God and your call to love.

The chapter closes with an urgent awakening. "The hour has come for you to wake up from your slumber." Paul is writing to Christians living in the already-not-yet tension of redemption—Christ has come, but His return is nearer than when they first believed. This awareness should transform how you live right now. The night is nearly over. The day has drawn near. This is not an invitation to escapism or to write off the present world as irrelevant. Rather, it is a call to live as if the kingdom of God is already breaking in, to lay aside the deeds of darkness—carousing, sexual immorality, dissension, jealousy—and to clothe yourself with the Lord Jesus Christ. Make no provision for the desires of the flesh. You are to live decently, honorably, as one who belongs to the coming day, not the passing night. Submission to government, love of neighbor, and holy living are not separate duties but expressions of a single conviction: that Jesus Christ is Lord, that His kingdom is real and advancing, and that every area of your life—civic, relational, personal—is to be surrendered to His rule.
